artifacts.content:
Mom,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m staring at this stupid spreadsheet again, so I figured I’d just write it all out to you instead of pacing the kitchen.

House is still sitting right around $325,000 from what Tom said after that job he did down the road, and I’ve got about $214,000 left on the mortgage. I keep reminding myself I’ve never missed a mortgage payment in almost nine years here on Cedar Elk Road, even when COVID hit and the clinic laid me off for a bit. That short-term tribal relief grant and my little emergency fund were the only reasons we stayed current back then.

Now it’s all these other pieces that are wearing me down. I’m juggling the $1,850 mortgage, $95 home insurance, $320 on the SUV with $7,800 still owed, plus the long-term Spokane Tribal Credit loan (about $8,900 left at $210/month) and the auto repair loan (around $1,300 left at $95/month). On top of that there’s about $11,200 in credit cards and that STCU roof loan.

You know the Chase Freedom is around $4,700 now from groceries, gas, your prescriptions at Sacred Heart’s pharmacy, and Maya’s ADHD meds at West Plains. The Capital One Quicksilver is roughly $2,300 from all those trips to Spokane for you and the kids. Then the STCU loan is still about $4,200 from the roof.

I keep telling myself if I can get everything into one sane payment, I’ll breathe again. I’m still putting a little into savings each month, even if it’s tiny, and whenever I get overtime or that tax refund, I throw it at the highest interest stuff. I just don’t want to ever be close to losing this house.

Love you,
Dani

Angie,

I needed to brain-dump to someone who actually gets all the family land craziness.

First, Dad’s 3‑acre trust parcel off Ford‑Wellpinit Road: even though probate wrapped back in March 2021 at Spokane Tribal Court and it’s officially split three ways between you, Tom, and me, my one‑third is still basically stuck. Mom’s been going in circles with the BIA Realty Office and the county over those 2018–2020 back property taxes since last summer, so nothing’s moving.

Then there’s the Drumheller Road mess. My name is still on the deed for that double‑wide outside Davenport we bought with Mark in June 2012. I left with the kids in November 2019 when his drinking got bad, but he’s the one paying the $475 lot rent at the mobile home park—when he remembers. He already got two eviction warnings in 2023. Every time my phone buzzes I half‑expect it to be some park manager asking about “our” trailer.

And I just found out last October that Lisa has Grandma and Grandpa’s old place on West Indiana Avenue in Spokane up on Airbnb. Mom showed me the listing. I still have a one‑quarter interest from when we updated the title at the Spokane County Recorder’s Office in 2016, and Lisa’s been renting it out informally since summer 2022. I’m freaking out about taxes and what happens if some drunk guest falls down the stairs.

Meanwhile, I’m sitting here stressing about replacing the windows and insulation on the only house that actually feels like home. I swear, between trust land, trailers, and surprise Airbnbs, owning just one boring, paid‑off house sounds like heaven.

Call me when you’re off shift.

Love,
Dani

Title: Money + family help – what I’m actually covering right now

Household basics:
- Mortgage: $1,850/month, balance ~$214,000 on Cedar Elk Rd. Never missed a payment in 9 years.
- Home insurance: $95/month (current, $0 balance).
- SUV loan: $320/month, about $7,800 left. Old sedan is paid off, worth around $6,500.
- Auto insurance: $110/month, 6‑month term, paid up.

Tribal + other loans:
- Spokane Tribal Credit long‑term: balance about $8,900, $210/month.
- Spokane Tribal Credit auto repair: balance about $1,300, $95/month.
- STCU roof loan: balance around $4,200.

Credit cards/other debt (total about $11,200):
- Chase Freedom Unlimited: ~$4,700 (groceries, gas, mom’s meds, Maya’s ADHD meds, Avista cash advance). Limit $12,000.
- Capital One Quicksilver: ~$2,300 (legal fees, emergencies). Limit $8,000.
- Other: CareCredit ($1,900 from Jan 2023 breast biopsy at Sacred Heart, $85/mo), Les Schwab tires/brakes, Best Buy for Evan’s laptop, plus a couple of medical collections and that $1,150 Spokane County District Court judgment from Aug 2023 at $50/mo.

Family obligations that don’t show on statements:
- Helping Mom with COPD/diabetes/heart meds from Sacred Heart/Providence: usually $250–$300/mo.
- Sending Angie ~$600/mo since May 2024 for rent on South 38th in Tacoma while she’s in outpatient at Sound.
- $100/mo toward Tom’s Northern Quest gambling collections so he doesn’t get dragged back into Spokane County Court.

Reminder to self: keep at least a tiny automatic transfer into savings every payday, even $25. When tax refund or overtime hits, throw it at Chase and Quicksilver first.

Tom,

I know you say I overthink everything, but I need you to see the whole picture of what I’m carrying so you don’t just tell me to “stop using the cards.”

On paper it doesn’t look awful: house worth about $325,000, mortgage balance around $214,000, two cars worth maybe $14,500 and $6,500 with only $7,800 left on the SUV loan. Total assets roughly $358,000, total liabilities around $244,200 when you add the mortgage, tribal loans, auto loan, and the $11,200 or so in credit cards and personal loans.

Reality feels different. The Spokane Tribal Credit long‑term loan still has about $8,900 left, the auto repair loan is at $1,300, and I’m paying $210 + $95 every month on top of the $1,850 mortgage and $320 car payment. Then there’s the Chase at about $4,700 and Capital One at $2,300, plus the STCU roof loan around $4,200.

I’m also quietly paying three different “ghost” things: $50/month on that $1,150 Spokane County District Court judgment from August 2023; $100/month toward your Northern Quest collections so they don’t haul you back into court; and $600/month to Angie in Tacoma since May 2024 so she doesn’t lose the 38th Street apartment while she’s at Sound Mental Health.

I’m proud I’ve never missed a mortgage payment and haven’t defaulted on anything, but keeping track of all these due dates is its own full‑time job. I still stash a little into savings and throw extra at the highest interest when I get overtime or a tax refund, but it feels like bailing a leaky boat with a coffee mug.

I just need you to understand why I’m tired all the time.

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: What the numbers really look like

artifacts.content:
Title: House projects + debt simplification plan – winter 2026

1. House as the anchor
- Current value: ~$325,000.
- Mortgage balance: about $214,000, payment $1,850/mo.
- Home insurance: $95/mo, current.
- Never been late on the mortgage, even during COVID layoff (used emergency fund + short‑term tribal relief).

2. Priority home improvements
- Replace aging, drafty windows (kids complain about cold air by their beds every winter).
- Improve insulation in walls + attic to cut heating costs.
- Roof already repaired with STCU loan (balance about $4,200 left from original $6,000).
- Talk with Tom again about which windows/insulation will give biggest energy savings.

3. Current major debts
- Spokane Tribal Credit long‑term: $8,900 balance, $210/mo.
- Spokane Tribal Credit auto repair: $1,300 balance, $95/mo.
- SUV loan: $7,800 balance, $320/mo.
- Credit cards + personal: about $11,200 total
  • Chase Freedom Unlimited – ~$4,700 (groceries, gas, meds, utilities).
  • Capital One Quicksilver – ~$2,300 (travel/emergencies).
  • STCU roof loan – ~$4,200.

4. Consolidation goals
- Roll higher‑interest debts (Chase, Cap One, STCU, maybe Les Schwab/Best Buy/CareCredit) into one fixed payment.
- Target: monthly payment that’s lower than current ~$475 on “credit cards/other debt” line so I can rebuild savings.
- Keep auto/home insurance separate (both paid in full each term, $0 balances).

5. Habits to keep
- Update color‑coded spreadsheet every Friday night after kids’ movie.
- Continue tiny automatic transfer to emergency fund.
- Any overtime or tax refund goes straight to principal on highest interest balance.

Reminder: House is my primary asset and the kids’ stability. Every decision has to protect that first.

Elena,

Catching up between no‑show patients and thought I’d answer your “how are you really?” text properly.

Most days I feel like a walking spreadsheet. I’ve got the house (worth about $325k, $214k still on the mortgage), two cars (one paid off, one with $7,800 left), the tribal long‑term loan (~$8,900), the auto repair loan (~$1,300), and about $11,200 spread across Chase, Capital One, and that STCU roof loan. On paper it’s manageable; in my brain it’s a swirl of due dates.

I keep thinking about last winter when Avista and Verizon were two months behind after I missed three weeks of work because the panic attacks got bad again. Dr. Harding at the Spokane Tribal Health Center helped me set up that $75‑every‑other‑Friday plan starting February 7, 2025, and I’ve stayed on top of it, but it scared me how fast things slipped.

Meanwhile I’m still sending Mom money for her COPD/diabetes/heart stuff from Sacred Heart and Providence, and I’ve been quietly helping Tom with $100/month toward that Northern Quest collections mess so he doesn’t end up back in Spokane County Court. Angie’s rent in Tacoma has been another $600/month since May while she’s at Sound Mental Health. None of that shows up anywhere except my checking balance.

The only thing that makes me feel halfway in control is that color‑coded budget I work on Friday nights while the kids watch a movie. I track every dollar, stash a tiny bit into savings, and whenever I get overtime or a tax refund I throw it at the highest‑interest card. It’s slow, but I haven’t missed a mortgage or loan payment yet.

Anyway, that’s the long answer. Coffee after clinic one of these days?

Dani

Event: Sunday dinner + money talk with Mom

Time: Sunday, January 12, 2026 – 3:00–7:30 p.m.
Location: Mom’s house, Drum Circle Lane, Spokane Reservation
Attendees: Me, Mom (Carol), Evan, Maya, maybe Tom & Angie

Notes:
- Bring printed copy of my latest budget spreadsheet (mortgage $1,850, home insurance $95, SUV loan $320 with $7,800 left, tribal long‑term loan $8,900 balance at $210/mo, tribal auto repair $1,300 at $95/mo, credit cards/personal loans ~$11,200).
- Go over house projects: new windows + insulation for Cedar Elk Rd place; remind Mom roof already handled with STCU loan (about $4,200 left from original $6k).
- Talk through all the “invisible” family money I’m helping with: her COPD/diabetes/heart meds from Sacred Heart/Providence (~$250–$300/mo), $600/mo to Angie in Tacoma since May 2024 for rent on South 38th while she’s in outpatient at Sound Mental Health, $100/mo toward Tom’s Northern Quest collections, plus the $150/mo I’m giving her back from the $2,450 she spotted me for Evan’s emergency dental surgery in Spokane Valley last July.
- Ask her honestly if she can scale back what she’s covering for me when Mark’s child support doesn’t show, so I stop feeling like I’m draining her Social Security.
- Non‑money stuff: have Evan show her his new basketball drills, get Maya to practice her dance routine in the living room, help Mom sort pill organizers for the week.

Reminder: leave by 7:30 so kids can shower and I can update spreadsheet before bed.

metadata.title: Sunday dinner at Mom’s – finances + family
